Quick overview of who we are, what we do, and why clients trust us.

The quick version

One Park Financial (OPF) is a business funding company founded in 2010, headquartered in Miami, Florida. We specialize in connecting small business owners with working capital — quickly, transparently, and without the red tape of traditional banks.

By the numbers

  • 55,000+ businesses funded to date
  • $1.5B+ in total capital deployed
  • 15+ years serving small businesses across the US
  • Operating in all 50 states + Puerto Rico

Trust & ratings

  • Trustpilot: 4.8 / 5
  • Google: 4.5 / 5
  • BBB: Accredited, A+ rating
  • Top Places to Work — 6 years running
  • Great Place to Work certified

Our mission

To become a partner in our customers' success through educating, encouraging, and mentoring small businesses — and helping them access the working capital they deserve.

On the call

When a client asks "Who is One Park Financial?" — lead with the stats (55K+ clients, $1.5B+ deployed, 15 years) and end with the ratings. Numbers build trust faster than descriptions.

A reference list of the business types OPF commonly funds.

We fund across virtually every industry

OPF works with small businesses across a wide range of sectors. If a client asks "Do you fund businesses like mine?" — the answer is almost always yes.

Common industries we serve

  • Restaurants & Food Service
  • Retail (brick-and-mortar and e-commerce)
  • Construction & Contractors
  • Healthcare & Medical Practices
  • Auto Repair & Dealerships
  • Transportation & Trucking
  • Beauty & Personal Care (salons, spas)
  • Professional Services (legal, accounting, consulting)
  • Cleaning & Janitorial Services
  • Landscaping & Lawn Care
  • Childcare & Education Services
  • Wholesale & Distribution
  • Manufacturing (light)
  • Home Services (HVAC, plumbing, electrical)

Restricted industries

Some industries may have additional underwriting requirements or may not qualify. If a client mentions cannabis, gambling, adult entertainment, or weapons — loop in your funding manager before continuing.

The four steps from application to money in the bank, and how fast each one moves.

The 4-step timeline

  • Step 1 — Pre-qualify: Takes just a few minutes on the phone or online. We ask about monthly revenue, time in business, and basic business info.
  • Step 2 — Assigned a Funding Manager: Once pre-qualified, the client is matched with a dedicated funding manager who guides them through the process.
  • Step 3 — Review the offer: Underwriting reviews the application and generates a funding offer. The client reviews terms, factor rate, and total payback.
  • Step 4 — Funded: Once the client accepts the offer, funds can be in their account in as little as 72 hours.

Say this

"From the time we finish this call to the time funds hit your account, we're talking as little as 72 hours. No waiting weeks. No back-and-forth with a bank. We move fast because we know your business can't always wait."

Contrast with banks

Traditional bank business loans can take 30–90 days and still result in a decline. Our process is designed for business owners who need capital now, not next quarter.

The three criteria every applicant must meet before we can move forward.

The three must-haves

  • 600+ FICO score — personal credit score of the business owner
  • $10,000+ monthly revenue — consistent monthly sales/revenue from the business
  • 3+ months in business — legally registered and actively operating

Additional notes

  • Business must be based in the United States or Puerto Rico
  • Must be a for-profit business
  • Personal guarantee is typically required from the primary owner

Do not guarantee approval

These are minimum thresholds, not approval guarantees. Final decisions depend on underwriting review. Never tell a client they are approved before underwriting confirms it.

Quick qualifier on the call

"Just to make sure we have options for you — can you tell me roughly what your monthly revenue looks like? And how long have you been in business?"

Required disclosure language and when it must be delivered.

Required disclosure

All calls must include the recording disclosure before any substantive business conversation takes place.

Required language

"I want to let you know that this call may be recorded for quality and training purposes. Is that okay with you?"

When to deliver it

Immediately after confirming the client can talk — before you begin discovery or discuss any product details.

Do not skip this

Skipping the recording disclosure is a compliance violation and may result in coaching or corrective action. It must be delivered on every call, every time.

If the client objects to being recorded

If a client refuses to be recorded, stop the call and escalate to your team lead immediately. Do not proceed with a sales conversation.

How to handle client financial and personal information securely during calls.

What counts as sensitive data

  • Social Security Numbers (SSN)
  • Employer Identification Numbers (EIN)
  • Bank account numbers and routing numbers
  • Personal financial information (revenue, debts, credit score)
  • Date of birth

Rules for handling sensitive data

  • Collect sensitive data only through authorized channels (Salesforce, secure forms)
  • Never write down SSNs or account numbers on paper or in unsecured notes
  • Never repeat sensitive numbers out loud unnecessarily — if a client gives you an SSN verbally, confirm by reading back only the last 4 digits
  • Do not share client information with anyone not involved in processing their file
  • Log all client information into Salesforce promptly and accurately

GLBA Compliance

OPF is subject to the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA), which governs how financial companies protect client information. Mishandling client data is a federal compliance issue — not just an internal policy violation.

Things you must never say on a client call — review before your first shift.

Never say these things

Guaranteed approval

Do NOT say: "You're approved," "You'll definitely qualify," or "We can guarantee you funding." Final approval comes from underwriting only. You can say "You may pre-qualify" or "Based on what you've told me, you look like a strong candidate."

APR quotes

Do NOT quote or calculate an APR equivalent for our factor-rate products. These are different financial instruments and an APR comparison is misleading. Only discuss factor rates and total payback amounts.

Specific funding amounts before underwriting

Do NOT tell a client they will receive a specific amount (e.g., "You'll get $75,000") before an offer has been generated by underwriting. Share ranges, not promises.

False competitor claims

Do NOT make false or unverifiable negative statements about any competitor. Comparative statements must be factually accurate. When in doubt, don't make the comparison.

Unauthorized use of "loan"

RBF products should not be described as "loans" in most contexts. Use "funding," "capital," "revenue-based financing," or "merchant cash advance" as appropriate for the product.

If you're unsure

When in doubt, don't say it. Escalate to your team lead or compliance contact before making any representation you're not 100% sure about.

Exploring stacking opportunities or positioning OPF for future funding cycles.

Don't concede — explore

Having a current lender doesn't mean they're fully funded or satisfied. Ask questions before accepting this as a closed door.

Say this

"That's great — how's that working out for you? Are you fully covered for what you need right now, or is there a gap?"

If there's a gap — stacking may apply

Depending on their existing balance and our underwriting guidelines, a client may qualify for additional capital stacked on top of their current position. Always loop in your funding manager before representing this as an option.

If they're fully funded

Say this

"Totally understand — I'm glad you're covered. When does your current position renew? I'd love to stay in touch so we can be on your radar when the time comes. A lot of our clients come back to us for their second and third rounds because of how smooth the process is."

Pro tip

Log the renewal date in Salesforce and set a follow-up task 30 days before their expected payoff. Being proactive before they go searching again is how you win the next round.

Addressing past bank rejections and positioning OPF's more flexible criteria.

Validate first

Getting declined by a bank is frustrating and demoralizing. Start by acknowledging that experience before pivoting to what's different about OPF.

Say this

"I'm really sorry that happened — and unfortunately it's more common than it should be. Traditional banks turn down a huge percentage of small business applications, often for reasons that have nothing to do with how well the business is actually doing."

Explain what's different

Continue with

"What we look at is different. We focus on your monthly revenue and business health — not just your credit score. If your business is generating $10,000 or more per month and you've been operating for at least 3 months, we have options for you."

OPF's minimum requirements (quick reference)

  • 600+ FICO score
  • $10,000+ in monthly revenue
  • 3+ months in business (legally registered)
  • Business located in the US or Puerto Rico

Pro tip

Ask "What was the reason they gave you?" — this helps you identify whether the client truly doesn't qualify or whether it was a bank-specific reason (e.g., no business credit history, industry restriction) that wouldn't apply to us.

"I don't want to go into debt"
Explaining how revenue-based financing differs from traditional debt instruments.

What they're really saying

The word "debt" carries emotional weight — especially for business owners who've seen others struggle. They're not objecting to capital; they're objecting to the risk they associate with fixed obligations. Revenue-based financing directly addresses that fear.

Say this

"I hear you — and that's exactly why revenue-based financing was built differently. You're not taking on a fixed loan payment that you owe regardless of how business goes. Your remittance adjusts with your revenue. Slower week? Smaller payment. Strong week? It pays down faster. You're always in control."

Key differentiators to mention

  • No fixed monthly payment that ignores your cash flow
  • No collateral required
  • No personal guarantee in most cases
  • Remittance is a small % of daily/weekly revenue — you pay as you earn

Pro tip

Use their own language back at them. If they said "I don't want to go into debt," say: "What you're describing is exactly what this isn't — it's a revenue partnership, not a fixed debt obligation."

How to keep the conversation alive when a prospect stalls and asks for more time.

Why they say it

"I need to think about it" almost never means the client is actually going away to research your offer. It usually signals one of three things: they don't have enough information, they feel mild pressure and need breathing room, or they haven't yet connected the funding to a real, urgent business need.

The goal

Don't fight the objection — agree with it, then ask a clarifying question that brings the conversation back to their need. You want to surface the real hesitation.

Say this

"Of course — this is a big decision and I want you to feel completely comfortable. While I have you, can I ask: what specific questions would you want answered in that thinking time? I'd love to make sure you have everything you need right now."

If they give you a reason

Now you have something to work with. Address the specific concern directly using the relevant objection guide (rates, spouse, past declines, etc.).

If they stay vague

Say this

"I totally understand. A lot of our clients felt the same way at first. What I can tell you is that the business owners who moved forward typically say the biggest regret was waiting. Can I ask — is there anything holding you back beyond just timing?"

Pro tip

Silence is your friend after asking a clarifying question. Give the client 3–5 seconds to respond before you speak again. Filling that silence yourself is the #1 mistake on stall objections.

If they insist on calling back

Secure the follow-up with specifics — not "I'll call you back," but a confirmed time.

Say this

"No problem at all. Let's set a time so I can hold your spot and make sure nothing changes in the meantime. Does [tomorrow at 10am] work, or is [Thursday afternoon] better?"

Reframing factor rates vs. APR, and shifting focus to the cost of not having capital.

Why this objection comes up

Most clients are comparing our factor rate to a bank's APR — two completely different structures. A bank APR of 8% sounds better than a 1.3 factor rate, but they're not comparable. Your job is to educate, not defend.

Important

Never quote an APR equivalent for our products. Factor rates and APR are different instruments. Quoting a converted APR is both misleading and a compliance violation.

Reframe: factor rate vs. APR

Say this

"I completely understand the concern — and I want to make sure we're comparing the right things. Our financing uses a factor rate, which works differently from a bank's APR. With a factor rate, you know your exact total payback from day one. There are no compounding surprises, no prepayment penalties. If you receive $50,000 at a 1.3 factor rate, your total payback is $65,000 — period."

Shift to the cost of not having capital

Say this

"Let me ask you something — what's the cost of not having this capital right now? If you're turning down orders, understaffed during your busy season, or watching a competitor take your customers — what does that cost your business per month?"

Speed premium

Banks can take 60–90 days and still say no. We can get funds in the client's account in 72 hours. That speed has value — especially when opportunity has a shelf life.

Pro tip

If the client has a specific number in mind ("I won't pay more than X%"), ask where that number came from. Often it's a bank quote they got months ago, or something they read online. That context helps you respond specifically.

Getting the decision-maker on the call or moving forward without them present.

Why they say it

This is a legitimate objection — many small businesses are family-run. The key is validating it without letting it become a dead end. Your goal is either to get the partner on the call now, or to arm your prospect with everything they need to "sell" it internally.

Say this (try to get them on now)

"Absolutely — big decisions should include everyone. Is there any chance your [husband/wife/partner] is available for just 10 minutes right now? I want to make sure they hear the same information and have their questions answered too."

If partner can't join now

Say this

"Totally understand. Let me ask — what would your partner's main questions be? Let me answer those now so you walk into that conversation fully prepared."

Common partner concerns to pre-address

  • What does it cost? → Explain the factor rate and total payback amount clearly.
  • What if business slows down? → Remittances flex with revenue — slower month = smaller payment.
  • Is this a loan? → RBF is structured differently. No fixed monthly payment, no collateral required.
  • Is OPF legitimate? → Trustpilot 4.8, BBB A+, Google 4.5, funded 55,000+ businesses since 2010.

Pro tip

Before ending the call, send them the OPF overview email so they have something tangible to share with their partner. Then lock in a specific callback time that includes the partner.

What quality evaluators look for and how calls are scored.

What gets evaluated

All calls are subject to quality review. Evaluators listen for:

  • Opening: Proper greeting, name, company, recording disclosure
  • Discovery: Qualifying questions asked, active listening demonstrated
  • Product knowledge: Accurate description of RBF, factor rates, and requirements
  • Objection handling: Professional, empathetic responses — no pressure tactics
  • Compliance: No prohibited statements, proper disclosures made
  • Closing: Clear next steps communicated, follow-up set if needed
  • Salesforce logging: Call logged within 5 minutes with accurate disposition and notes

Metrics you'll be tracked on

  • Contact rate (% of leads successfully reached)
  • Conversion rate (% of contacted leads moved to funding manager)
  • Average handle time (target varies by team — ask your lead)
  • QA score (monthly average from call reviews)

Fastest way to improve QA scores

Record yourself on a practice call and listen back. Most agents hear things they didn't realize they were saying — filler words, rushing, or skipping the disclosure. Self-review once a week is the single highest-leverage habit for score improvement.

What gets evaluated

All calls are subject to quality review. Evaluators listen for:

  • Opening: Proper greeting, name, company, recording disclosure
  • Discovery: Qualifying questions asked, active listening demonstrated
  • Product knowledge: Accurate description of RBF, factor rates, and requirements
  • Objection handling: Professional, empathetic responses — no pressure tactics
  • Compliance: No prohibited statements, proper disclosures made
  • Closing: Clear next steps communicated, follow-up set if needed
  • Salesforce logging: Call logged within 5 minutes with accurate disposition and notes

Metrics you'll be tracked on

  • Contact rate (% of leads successfully reached)
  • Conversion rate (% of contacted leads moved to funding manager)
  • Average handle time (target varies by team — ask your lead)
  • QA score (monthly average from call reviews)

Fastest way to improve QA scores

Record yourself on a practice call and listen back. Most agents hear things they didn't realize they were saying — filler words, rushing, or skipping the disclosure. Self-review once a week is the single highest-leverage habit for score improvement.
The minimum Salesforce actions you need to take on every call.

Before the call

  • Open the lead record in Salesforce and review any prior notes or call history
  • Check the lead source and any pre-filled data from the web form
  • Note any prior contact attempts and outcomes

During the call

  • Take notes in real-time in the Salesforce activity log (or your note pad if typing is distracting)
  • Do not leave Salesforce fields blank — partial data is better than no data

After every call — required fields to update

  • Call Disposition: Connected / No Answer / Left Voicemail / Wrong Number / etc.
  • Lead Status: Update to reflect where the client is in the funnel
  • Next Activity Date: Set a follow-up task if the client needs a callback
  • Notes: Summary of the conversation, objections raised, client's stated need, and any commitments made
  • Monthly Revenue: If collected during discovery
  • Industry: If not already populated

Log within 5 minutes

Memory degrades fast. Log call notes while the conversation is fresh — before your next call comes in. Your funding manager reads these notes to continue the relationship; your accuracy directly impacts their success.

Every platform you'll use and what each one is for.

Your daily toolkit

  • Salesforce — CRM. All lead data, call logs, and pipeline management live here. This is your single source of truth.
  • RingCentral — Phone system. Used for all inbound and outbound client calls. Calls are recorded automatically.
  • Knowledge Base (this app) — Your go-to resource for quick answers during calls. Search by keyword or browse by category.
  • Slack — Internal communication. Use it to reach your team lead, funding managers, or compliance when you have a question mid-call that you can't answer yourself.
  • DocuSign — You won't use this directly, but clients will receive agreements via DocuSign. Know that this is how they sign.

Two-monitor workflow tip

Keep Salesforce on your primary monitor and the Knowledge Base on your secondary. This lets you reference resources without losing sight of the client's record. Set this up on your first day.

How we deliver funding so quickly — and what the client needs to do to keep things moving.

What "72 hours" really means

Once a client accepts their offer and all required documents are submitted, funds can be deposited into their business bank account in as little as 72 business hours. This clock starts from offer acceptance — not from the first call.

What the client needs to do to keep it moving

  • Respond promptly to document requests from their funding manager
  • Provide accurate bank statements (typically 3–6 months)
  • Ensure their business bank account is active and accessible for ACH
  • Sign the agreement electronically (DocuSign)

Common delays

The biggest cause of delays is slow document submission. If a client wants their money in 72 hours, they need to be responsive. Let them know speed is a two-way street.

Set expectations on the call

"We move fast on our end — our goal is to have an offer in front of you quickly. The key is that when your funding manager reaches out for documents, you respond the same day. The faster you move, the faster funds hit your account."

General guidance on how much a client might qualify for based on monthly revenue.

General range

OPF's funding amounts generally range from $5,000 to $500,000+, though the actual offer depends on underwriting review of the client's specific financials.

How amounts are determined

Underwriting considers several factors:

  • Average monthly gross revenue (primary driver)
  • Time in business
  • Credit profile
  • Industry and business type
  • Existing debt obligations
  • Bank account health (consistency, no chronic overdrafts)

Rough rule of thumb

Clients can often qualify for 50–150% of their average monthly revenue. A business doing $30,000/month might see offers between $15,000–$45,000 at the start of the relationship. Higher amounts become available with a successful funding history.

Don't promise specific amounts

Never tell a client they qualify for a specific dollar amount before underwriting reviews their file. You can share ranges, but final offers come from underwriting only.

Remittance schedules, factor rates, and what clients experience during repayment.

The remittance structure

Once funded, the client's remittance is set as a fixed percentage of their daily or weekly gross revenue — collected automatically via ACH from their business bank account.

Example breakdown

  • Funded amount: $50,000
  • Factor rate: 1.30
  • Total payback: $65,000
  • Daily remittance rate: ~8–12% of daily revenue (varies by deal)
  • Estimated term: 6–12 months (depends on revenue volume)

Why the "term" is an estimate

Because remittance is revenue-based, there's no fixed end date. A business with a great quarter pays off faster. A slower period means smaller payments — but no penalty for it.

What happens on a slow day?

If the client's daily revenue is low, their daily remittance is proportionally lower. This is the core flexibility benefit of RBF vs. a fixed loan payment.

ACH and bank account

Clients must have an active business checking account for ACH remittance. Personal accounts or accounts with frequent overdrafts may cause issues in underwriting.

A side-by-side comparison to help clients understand why OPF is different.

Side-by-side comparison

OPF (RBF) Traditional Bank Loan
Time to fund As little as 72 hours 30–90+ days
Minimum credit score 600+ FICO 700+ typically
Collateral Not required Often required
Fixed monthly payment No — adjusts with revenue Yes — fixed regardless of revenue
Time in business required 3+ months 2+ years typically
Application process Simple, minimal paperwork Extensive documentation
Approval rate Higher — revenue-focused Low — strict criteria

When to use this comparison

Pull this out when a client says "I was going to just go to my bank" or "Can't I just get an SBA loan?" It makes the practical difference immediate and concrete.

How to end the call with a clear commitment and move the client toward funded.

The soft close

Never end a call without a clear next action — agreed to by the client. Even if they're not ready to accept an offer today, there should be a specific follow-up commitment.

After presenting the offer

"Based on everything we've talked about — does this sound like something you'd like to move forward with today?"

If yes — launch the process

Say this

"Perfect. I'm going to transfer you to your dedicated funding manager right now. They'll walk you through the next steps and get the documentation process started. You could have funds in your account in as little as 72 hours."

If they need more time

Say this

"No problem. I'll hold your information and we can reconnect when you're ready. Can we schedule a specific time — how does [tomorrow at 2pm] work?"

Always log the outcome

Immediately after the call, update Salesforce with the call disposition, next steps, and any notes about objections or client-specific context. Your funding manager depends on this to pick up where you left off.

The key questions to ask early in the call to understand the client's situation and needs.

The discovery framework

Discovery has two goals: qualify the lead against our minimums, and understand the "why" behind their funding need. The "why" shapes your pitch and helps you connect capital to a real business outcome.

Core qualifying questions

  • "What type of business do you have, and how long have you been operating?"
  • "What's your average monthly revenue looking like?"
  • "Are you the owner or co-owner of the business?"
  • "Do you have a business checking account in the company name?"

Need-uncovering questions

  • "What are you looking to use the funding for?"
  • "Is there a specific opportunity or project driving the timing?"
  • "Have you explored other financing options? What happened?"
  • "If you had the capital available tomorrow, what would you do first?"

Listen for the urgency

The best leads have a specific, time-sensitive use case — a new contract they need to staff up for, an equipment purchase before busy season, inventory for a big order. Urgency accelerates decisions. If you find it, reflect it back to them throughout the call.

How to open an inbound call — introduce yourself, confirm the lead, and set the agenda.

Purpose

The first 30 seconds set the tone. Your goal is to be warm, professional, and immediately signal that this call is worth the client's time.

Standard opening

"Hi, this is [Your Name] calling from One Park Financial. I'm reaching out because you recently expressed interest in business funding — is now a good time to talk for a few minutes?"

If they say yes — confirm and set the agenda

"Perfect. I just want to ask you a few quick questions to see what options might be available for your business. It'll only take about 5 minutes, and I'll give you a straight answer on what we can do. Sound good?"

If they say it's a bad time

Say this

"No problem at all — I'll be quick. When is a good time to reach you? I want to make sure I have time set aside specifically for you."

Pro tip

Always ask "Is now a good time?" before launching into your pitch. It respects the client's time, reduces resistance, and if they say yes — they've subconsciously committed to the conversation.

Call recording disclosure

After the client confirms they can talk, include the required disclosure:

Required

"I also want to let you know that this call may be recorded for quality and training purposes — is that okay with you?"

A plain-language explanation of how RBF works — for you and for your clients.

The simple version

Revenue-Based Financing (RBF) is a way for a business to receive a lump sum of capital upfront in exchange for agreeing to remit a small, fixed percentage of their daily or weekly revenue until the total agreed amount is paid back.

How it's different from a loan

  • No fixed monthly payment — remittance adjusts with your revenue
  • No compounding interest — uses a factor rate (a flat multiplier), not APR
  • No collateral required — based on business revenue, not assets
  • Faster to fund — as little as 72 hours vs. weeks or months at a bank

The factor rate explained

Instead of interest, RBF uses a factor rate — a simple multiplier applied once to the funded amount.

  • Example: $50,000 funded × 1.3 factor rate = $65,000 total payback
  • The client knows exactly what they owe from day one
  • No surprises, no compounding

Client-friendly explanation

"Think of it this way: we give you $50,000 today, and over time you pay back $65,000 through a small daily percentage of your revenue. On a slow day you pay less, on a great day you pay a bit more — but it's always proportional to what's coming in. You're never stuck with a fixed bill you can't afford."

How to walk a client through a funding offer clearly and confidently.

Before you present

Make sure you understand the offer details yourself before walking the client through it. Know the funded amount, factor rate, total payback, and estimated daily/weekly remittance.

Structure for presenting

  1. Anchor with the funded amount — lead with what they get
  2. Explain the total payback — what they owe overall
  3. Describe the remittance — how it gets paid back (daily/weekly % of revenue)
  4. Tie it to their goal — connect the capital to what they told you they need it for
  5. Ask for the next step — don't wait for them to drive

Full offer presentation template

"So based on what we looked at, here's what we can offer you: [Amount] deposited directly into your business account. The total payback on that would be [Total Payback], and that gets collected through a small daily percentage of your revenue — so you're not locked into a fixed payment that ignores how business is going. Given what you told me about [their specific goal], this should give you everything you need to [tie to their goal]. What questions do you have about the offer?"

Don't apologize for the terms

Present the offer with confidence. If you say "The rates are a little high but..." you've already planted doubt. Present the numbers factually, then pause and let the client respond. Confidence is contagious.
